---
title: "How to search Ubuntu forums:- (search help)"
date: 2014-04-17
forum: Forum Feedback &amp; Help
---

### Post by Ace..... on 2014-04-17
[SIZE=4]**How to search Ubuntu forums:- (search help)**[/SIZE]

Help on how to search Ubuntu forums, to instantly find the documents or information that relates to your needs.
> Note: Do not use the search box at the top of each page.
It simply does not work, and** it is not going to be fixed**.
(as vBulletin software, it is outside the control of Ubuntu forum members).


Follow the instructions below, and the entire Ubuntu Forums Repository of computing knowledge will be available to you at the click of an icon.


[SIZE=3]**User Guide to Ubuntu Forums Search**[/SIZE]

[LIST=1]
[*]Load a google search page.
[COLOR=#ffffff].[/COLOR]
[*]Enter or type:
site:ubuntuforums.org.
[COLOR=#ffffff].[/COLOR]
[*]Action the search.
[COLOR=#ffffff].[/COLOR]
[*]Click 'bookmark' and save to your 'bookmark bar'
[COLOR=#ffffff].[/COLOR]
[*]Right-click and edit (or edit through bookmark manager):
The name will have been saved as "site:ubuntuforums.org." so edit the name to just Ubuntu (leaving the url untouched).
[COLOR=#ffffff].[/COLOR]
[*]You should now have a blue and white 'g' google icon on your bookmark bar (followed by the name 'Ubuntu').
If you can't see this icon.... bookmark manager will allow you to drag this link higher up the list until it displays on your browser bookmark bar.
If you can't see any of your bookmarks on your browser menu.... consider enabling 'bookmark bar' under menu item 'view'.
[/LIST]

**Once this preparatory work is completed**.... to search Ubuntu forums one need only **click the 'google ubuntu' icon**, and enter ones search data (preceded by a space, after site:ubuntuforums.org).

**IE. Simply enter the words most relevant to your query.**
eg.```
 site:ubuntuforums.org. re-install ubuntu
```

Using this example, we gain a very good mix of results, all of which relate in different ways to the search query.[INDENT]*(don't bother trying to search "re-install ubuntu" using Ubuntu search...... only non-relevant results are posted.)*[/INDENT]

**Note:-** As a fundamental computer information resource, I believe it is important to have access to Ubuntu Forum resources at a single click.
The objective of this search technique is not to keep you off the forums, but rather to 'link you to the information you need, at the moment you need it'.
You may still need to post a question; but hopefully your question will be better informed from the information you have already gained.

**Special Note:-**
In all my years using Ubuntu forums, I never knew how to enable 'Ubuntu search' in my browser, and of course, searching for information using the Ubuntu Search  was 'nigh on impossible'.
My belief is that there are a vast number of community members, who also are unaware of this feature, not to mention all the newcomers to the Ubuntu operating system.......[SIZE=2]** how are they going to know about this if they are not told?**[/SIZE]

[SIZE=3]**So what next?**[/SIZE]
Perhaps 'peer review'?

**Try any number of searches:** using the 'Ubuntu search box', and try the same searches using the above method.
The 'above method' works every time.......

......'Ubuntu Search' fails every time.

[SIZE=3]**So what next?**[/SIZE]
Implementing a solution seems to be the best option.

_[SIZE=3]**The Solution**[/SIZE]_

In its simplest form: 
Replace the 'Ubuntu Search Box' with a new search box that has "site:ubuntuforums.org. " pre-coded to the inputted search text.

In this way, the user need only enter their search query to achieve what they require.

A more complex format might be to contain the google generated results within an 'Ubuntu forum' document format.

[SIZE=3]**Is This Bug Reportable?**[/SIZE]

It is not related to ubuntu software.
The problem lies with vBulletin software (the underlying forum o/s).

This problem was initially raised under 'forum feedback' and **SeijiSensei** stated:
> On another vBulletin forum I frequent, the admin replaced the vB search engine with Sphinx. It's a very impressive search tool that is designed specifically to index materials kept in a database like vBulletin does. Of course, that required some major surgery on the vB code.

Admirable though that effort must have been - and why not climb that mountain if the desire is there - but we do clearly have an instant(?) solution that is guaranteed to deliver the results pertinent to the users need.

In reality, the choice is so stark, one would question the cause of inactivity, once the solution has been proffered.

What do YOU think?


[I]**Background Note:**
This solution resulted from _[[COLOR=#0000ff]this thread posted under 'forum feedback'[/COLOR]]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=2213097")_.
Many thanks to** bapoumba** for highlighting the use of "site:ubuntuforums.org. " in google search.
[/I]

:)

---

### Post by 23dornot23d on 2014-04-17
Same here the results in UF are so out of line with whatever we put in to search for .......... 

That its just not worth the hassle of trying to work out what on earth its trying to do ........ even on one word searches

So I just go to google often just using ( Ubuntu Solved "keyword/s" 2014 ) >>> sorted

If Ubuntu had one simple search .... where a one liner like that gave back the correct results - then I would use it .........

___________________________________________________

No idea which field or how to get the up to date results using UF search engine .

Ubuntu solved nvidia 2014

[http://i.minus.com/ibh9jnDFVfJdWj.png](http://i.minus.com/ibh9jnDFVfJdWj.png)

### Post by Elfy on 2014-04-18

### Post by Ace..... on 2014-04-22
[SIZE=3]**The question resolves down to:**[/SIZE] whether there exists a large proportion of ubuntu users; and particularly 'new to ubuntu' users, that do not know this very simple search trick.

I've been working with PC's since IBM DOS and bizarrely I never knew this search trick.

I'm guessing I'm not alone.
In fact I'm guessing that there are huge numbers of people who don't know this trick.

Looking at a normal curve; my guess (could be wrong) would be that two thirds of users will not know this trick.
(since learning it I have now used it many times).

[SIZE=3][B]So what do we know? 
[/B][SIZE=2]
We know that the Ubuntu search box doesn't work
We know that the suggested search methodology works _perfectly_ (it really does and can be tested).

[SIZE=3]**So What Next?**[/SIZE]

The simple guide that I prepared, could be uploaded to a tutorial section.
However, for the newbie to find it, they would need to search for it [/SIZE][/SIZE]:p

The perfect definition of a **'Catch 22'** scenario.
You need to read the 'search help doc' but in order to find it, you first need to read the 'search help doc'. :)

Therefore...... this 'search help doc' needs to be a sticky in, at least, General Help.
But.... in truth.... because the current search method _DOES NOT WORK_ the ideal scenario for Ubuntu forum users, would be to eliminate the current failed search box, and replace it with a search box pre-coded with:

'site:ubuntuforums.org. '

In this way, there would be no need for a search help document.
People would simply search as they wish to search..... and they would get very good results.

This seems a 'no-brainer' to me.

[B]Would there be any negatives from doing this?
Would there be any positives from NOT doing this?[/B]

:)

---

### Post by vasa1 on 2014-04-22
> **ibjsb4 said:**
> Googlubuntu has been down for two or three weeks :(

I made my own version: [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=2216408&p=12984962#post12984962](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=2216408&p=12984962#post12984962)

### Post by Elfy on 2014-04-23
> **ibjsb4 said:**
> Googlubuntu has been down for two or three weeks :(

I tend to default to my other one - the #! engine.

Save it to the searchplugin folder in firefox - call it whatever you want.
```
<SearchPlugin xmlns="http://www.mozilla.org/2006/browser/search/" xmlns:os="http://a9.com/-/spec/opensearch/1.1/">
<os:ShortName>Doobiewotsit</os:ShortName>
<os:Description>Search the Ubuntu Universe for all things Ubuntu related.</os:Description>
<os:InputEncoding>UTF-8</os:InputEncoding>
<os:Image width="16" height="16">data:image/x-icon;base64,</os:Image>
<SearchForm>http://crunchbang.org/ubuntu-search-engine/</SearchForm>
<os:Url type="text/html" method="GET" template="http://www.google.com/cse">
  <os:Param name="cx" value="012285703143635244993:i9yr8qlpb18"/>
  <os:Param name="q" value="{searchTerms}"/>
</os:Url>
</SearchPlugin>

```

---

### Post by ajgreeny on 2014-04-25
I have set up my own custom google search site which I,ll link here later when back on my main machine. It uses this forum, askubuntu, ubuntu.com, launchpad and the wiki as sources and seems to work very well. Link to come later for those interested.

Here's the link to the custom google search.  I hope it's as useful to some others as it is to me.
[https://www.google.co.uk/cse/publicurl?cx=003847201077524938900:ukjniuf1wao](https://www.google.co.uk/cse/publicurl?cx=003847201077524938900:ukjniuf1wao)

### Post by Ace..... on 2014-04-26
> **ajgreeny said:**
> 
Here's the link to the custom google search.  I hope it's as useful to some others as it is to me.
[https://www.google.co.uk/cse/publicurl?cx=003847201077524938900:ukjniuf1wao](https://www.google.co.uk/cse/publicurl?cx=003847201077524938900:ukjniuf1wao)

It definitely works.
There are minor differences with 
site:ubuntuforums.org.
However, there is no need to add a 'space' at the start of your query.

It also has the advantage of 'looking the part'.

Should it be 'this' that replaces the current Ubuntu search?

---

### Post by ajgreeny on 2014-04-27
> **Ace..... said:**
> It definitely works.
There are minor differences with 
site:ubuntuforums.org.
However, there is no need to add a 'space' at the start of your query.

It also has the advantage of 'looking the part'.

Should it be 'this' that replaces the current Ubuntu search?
I'm glad that you find it helpful.
The other main difference from using the **site:ubuntuforums.org** search method is that my custom search is for those other sites as well as the forum, so therefore a bit wider in its findings.

### Post by Ace..... on 2014-04-30
So..... Should this:

[https://www.google.co.uk/cse/publicurl?cx=003847201077524938900:ukjniuf1wao](https://www.google.co.uk/cse/publicurl?cx=003847201077524938900:ukjniuf1wao)

be the search to replace the current search box?

OR
provided as an alternative

OR
offered as part of a help file, launched via a link next to the current search box?

---

### Post by ajgreeny on 2014-05-01
> **Ace..... said:**
> So..... Should this:

[https://www.google.co.uk/cse/publicurl?cx=003847201077524938900:ukjniuf1wao](https://www.google.co.uk/cse/publicurl?cx=003847201077524938900:ukjniuf1wao)

be the search to replace the current search box?

OR
provided as an alternative

OR
offered as part of a help file, launched via a link next to the current search box?
Thanks for your "vote of confidence" re this custom search engine.  Anyone could have set it up for themselves, and could easily do another if they wanted using different sites.  I only set it up because another customised search engine, [googlubuntu]("http://www.googlubuntu.com/") which I used to use a lot disappeared a couple of weeks ago and looked as if it might never reappear, however, yesterday it was back online, so now I (and you) have two options at least for searching ubuntu related info.

---

### Post by Ace..... on 2014-05-01
> **ajgreeny said:**
> Thanks for your "vote of confidence" re this custom search engine.  Anyone could have set it up for themselves, and could easily do another if they wanted using different sites.  I only set it up because another customised search engine, [googlubuntu]("http://www.googlubuntu.com/") which I used to use a lot disappeared a couple of weeks ago and looked as if it might never reappear, however, yesterday it was back online, so now I (and you) have two options at least for searching ubuntu related info.

I am grateful for this information.
I like the way that you combined all ubuntu community resources into a searchable repository.

This opens up simply ENORMOUS potential.

Imagine you have 10 used parts suppliers for a re-build project.
A general search would produce a huge amount of crap.
Custom search saved to bookmark would provide you with instant, perfectly relevant results (etc. etc.)

**However....**

A user, arriving at ubuntu forums, doesn't immediately want to start designing his custom search box.
What they desperately (usually they are desperate) want to do, is search for information that is relevant to their needs.

We have already shown that the current search system is a total failure - apparently broken beyond repair.

 So:[INDENT][COLOR=#000000]**1. Should this universal 'ubuntu resource' search, replace the current search box?**[/COLOR][/INDENT]
[COLOR=#000000]OR[/COLOR][INDENT]**[COLOR=#000000]2. Should it be provided as an alternative?[/COLOR]**[/INDENT]
[COLOR=#000000]OR[/COLOR][INDENT][COLOR=#000000]**3. Should it be offered as part of a help file, launched via a link next to the current search box?**
[/COLOR][/INDENT]
[COLOR=#000000]OR[INDENT]**4. Does anybody have any better suggestions (re: upgrading ubuntu search)?**[/INDENT]

:)[/COLOR]

---
